issues.problem: Slightly misleading because `unimproveHash` is identical to `improveHash` (as the doc comment itself notes), so it does not actually recover the original hash — it applies the same XOR transformation again. The phrase 'original hash before improvement' could lead readers to believe it is a true inverse.
issues.suggestion: the hash index recovered by re-applying the improvement transformation (which happens to be its own inverse)

issues.current_text: the improved hash with better entropy in the low-order bits
issues.problem: Accurate but could be more precise about the mechanism. Minor style point only.
issues.suggestion: the improved hash, with high bits XORed into the low bits to spread entropy into the bits used for bucket indexing

issues.current_text: the improved hash of the element
issues.problem: Adequate but slightly repetitive with the method description. Could mention that it uses `##` followed by `improveHash`.
issues.suggestion: the improved hash of `o`, computed from `o.##`

issues.current_text: `true` if the element was added, or `false` if it was already present
issues.problem: Clear and accurate; minor wording tweak only.
issues.suggestion: `true` if `elem` was added to the set, or `false` if an equal element was already present

general_feedback: The filled-in @return descriptions are accurate, concise, and consistent with the surrounding Scaladoc style (lowercase start, brief one-line form, backticks for `true`/`false`). The only notable concern is the `unimproveHash` @return wording — the implementation calls `improveHash` again rather than truly inverting it, so 'the original hash before improvement' is technically misleading even though the method comment explains the involution. The other suggestions are minor stylistic polish.
